registry.description: Grief is the inner response to genuine loss — the pain of bereavement, devastation, and the experience of something or someone precious being gone. The Hebrew vocabulary is among the richest in the corpus, spanning the darkness of mourning, the bitterness of affliction, and the sorrow of a broken heart. God himself grieves in Scripture: he is grieved by the wickedness of humanity (Genesis 6), the breaking of the relationship he made. Grief is honoured in the Bible rather than suppressed; it is the appropriate inner response to a world where real loss happens.

cross_registry_links.note: Isa 63:10 uses a.tsav to describe Israel grieving the Holy Spirit — a theologically significant connection relevant to the Holy Spirit project.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: The registries are connected by a theological concept or theme

cross_registry_links.note: Eph 4:30 — 'do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God' — uses lu.pe.o (G3076) as the verb. This is the NT counterpart to Isa 63:10 (a.tsav, Part 2) and constitutes a direct Hebrew-Greek correspondence on the theme of grieving the Spirit. Theologically significant for the Holy Spirit project.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: The registries are connected by a theological concept or theme

cross_registry_links.note: ra.ah (H7451C) appears in anger-related contexts and shares semantic range with anger/wrath word sets.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: The registries share a common etymological root

cross_registry_links.note: ka.a.s (H3708B) and ka.as (H3708A) both carry primary meanings of anger and provocation alongside grief, placing them at the intersection of the grief and anger word sets. Verses such as 1Ki 15:30 and 2Ki 23:26 concern divine anger provoked by Israel.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: The two registries share overlapping meaning space

cross_registry_links.note: a.tsav (H6087A) appears in anger-proximate contexts (Gen 34:7 — indignation; 1Sa 20:34 — fierce anger) and its meaning range includes displease and vex, overlapping with anger terminology.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: The two registries share overlapping meaning space

cross_registry_links.note: o.ni (affliction/misery) overlaps semantically with the anguish word set.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: The two registries share overlapping meaning space

cross_registry_links.note: ya.gon (H3015) is defined as grief, sorrow, anguish, creating direct semantic overlap with any anguish word set. Psa 116:3 records both distress and anguish in the same verse.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: The two registries share overlapping meaning space

cross_registry_links.note: o.du.ne (G3601) is glossed 'anguish' and has direct meaning overlap with any anguish word set. Rom 9:2 pairs it with lu.pe (G3077) — great sorrow and unceasing anguish — in the same verse.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: The two registries share overlapping meaning space

cross_registry_links.note: o.ni (H6040) shares root a.nah with a.nav (poor/humble) and an.vah (gentleness/humility), creating a link between affliction and humility word sets.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: The registries share a common etymological root

cross_registry_links.note: Matt 5:4 — 'Blessed are those who mourn' — places mourning (pen.the.o) within the Beatitudes alongside poverty of spirit (Matt 5:3), linking grief-mourning to humility and poverty-of-spirit word sets.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: The two registries share overlapping meaning space

cross_registry_links.note: Jam 4:9-10 pairs pen.the.o/penthos directly with the command to humble oneself before God, setting mourning as the antithesis of pride and arrogance. Rev 18:7 contrasts penthos with self-glorification.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: The registries represent antonyms or semantic contrasts

cross_registry_links.note: Jer 20:18 pairs sorrow (ya.gon) with shame in a single lament, connecting the grief and shame word sets.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: Terms from the two registries frequently appear together in passages

cross_registry_links.note: Isa 53:3-4 presents cho.li (grief/sickness) in the context of the Suffering Servant who was despised and rejected, with implicit shame and social rejection. Cross-connects grief and shame word sets through the Servant Songs.
cross_registry_links.link_type_description: Terms from the two registries frequently appear together in passages

__2 in singular also, that which is born amid throes, child, παῖδα, φιλτάτην ἐμοὶ ὠδῖνα [Refs 5th c.BC+]; θαλλὸν ἱερὸν ἐλαίας, Λατοῦς ὠδῖνα (perhaps ὠδῖνι) φίλαν [Refs]; ἄπτερον ὠδῖνα τέκνων, of young birds, [Refs]: plural, children, [Refs 2nd c.AD+]; ὀρταλίχων ἁπαλὴ ὠδίς, of eggs, [Refs 4th c.BC+]; ὠ. Θαλάσσας, of Aphrodite, [Refs]; ὠδὶς μελίσσης, of honey, [Refs 4th c.AD+]
__II metaphorically, travail, anguish, [Refs 4th c.BC+]: also in plural, of love, ἐμοὶ πικρὰς ὠδῖνας αὑτοῦ προσβαλὼν ἀποίχεται [NT+5th c.BC+]
__II.2 fruit of the mind's travail, τῆς ἐμῆς ὠ. [Refs 2nd c.AD+]
__II.3 ὠδῖνες θανάτου, ᾅδου, the bonds of death, [LXX] (due to confusion of Heb. [hudot]ēbel 'pang' with [hudot]èbel 'cord'),compare [NT]

senses.sense_text:
labor, birth pain; agony (of death) 
the spasms, or pains, of a woman in travail, a birth pang, 1Thess. 5:3; plural metaphorically birth throes, preliminary troubles to the development of a catastrophe, Mt. 24:8; Mk. 13:8; from the Hebrew, a stringent band, a snare, noose, Acts 2:24*
